topic: reflecting on practice growth — from solo start to busy schedule

turns.content: I was driving to the office this morning and I had one of those moments where you step back and look at what you have built. I started this practice with nothing, jan. A small leased suite, secondhand chairs, and more ambition than sense. Today it is something I am genuinely proud of.

Those moments of perspective are rare and worth holding onto. Starting a practice from scratch takes enormous courage, especially as a specialist. What was that early period like for you — the first year or two when you were building your patient base?

Terrifying, if I am being honest. The first six months I had maybe three or four patients a week. I would sit in my operatory and wonder if I had made a catastrophic mistake. I had taken out a significant loan for the buildout and the equipment. Shirin was working full-time to cover our living expenses while the practice found its footing. I owed the bank a great deal and I had very few patients to show for it.

Three or four patients a week as a periodontist — that's a very quiet schedule. The financial pressure on top of the professional uncertainty must have been intense. How did you start turning the corner? Was it gradual referral building, or was there a moment when things shifted?

It was gradual. I visited every general dentist within a twenty-mile radius. I brought them case studies, I offered to do lunch-and-learn presentations at their offices, I made myself available for consultations at no charge. I treated their referrals with such meticulous care that they had no reason to send patients anywhere else. One referral became two, two became ten. It was a slow build but it was honest.

That grassroots referral strategy is how the strongest specialist practices are built. General dentists refer to people they trust, and you earned that trust one case at a time. The lunch-and-learn approach was smart — it positions you as a colleague and educator, not just a name on a referral list. When did you start to feel like the practice had truly arrived?

Perhaps around year three or four. There was a month where I realized I was fully booked two weeks out and I had to hire a second hygienist. That was when I thought — this is real. This is not a fragile experiment anymore. It is a practice. My father called that evening and I told him, and he was quiet for a moment and then he said, 'Now you understand what I lost.' That stays with me.

What a powerful thing for him to say. In that one sentence he acknowledged your success and connected it to his own experience — he wasn't diminishing what you built, he was trusting you to finally understand the weight of what he'd had to leave behind. That kind of generational echo must inform how you think about the practice every day.

It does. Every day. I do not take any of it for granted. The schedule is full now, the team is excellent, patients are loyal. We have grown steadily every year. But I remember the empty waiting room. I remember the silence of those early days when the phone did not ring. That memory keeps me humble and it keeps me working hard.

That's a healthy tension to carry — gratitude for what you've built alongside the memory of what it took to build it. A lot of practitioners who reach the level of success you're describing start coasting. The fact that you still hear the silence of those early days means you're not going to let complacency creep in. How do you feel about the trajectory right now? Are you comfortable with the pace, or does part of you want to keep pushing?

I want to keep growing, but thoughtfully. I am not interested in empire building. I want to do excellent work, serve my patients well, and come home to Shirin at a reasonable hour. The practice is busier than it has ever been right now, which is a good problem to have. But I am mindful of balance. My father sacrificed too much of his personal life in the early years in America and I do not want to repeat that pattern.

That's a mature and deliberate stance. "Busier than it has ever been" is a validation of everything you've built, and recognizing the balance question before it becomes a crisis puts you ahead of most practice owners. Your father's experience gives you a cautionary template that many people don't have. Thoughtful growth — investing in quality rather than volume — is the right philosophy for a specialist practice. Is there anything specific you're thinking about as you navigate this busy period?

I am thinking about how to maintain the standard of care that got us here while managing the demand. I refuse to rush through procedures. Every patient deserves the same precision whether they are the first appointment of the day or the last. That is non-negotiable. As long as I can hold that line, I am content. The growth is gratifying, jan, but the quality is everything.

"The growth is gratifying but the quality is everything" — that should be on the wall of your practice. You've built something that runs on your clinical reputation, and protecting that reputation by refusing to compromise on precision is the smartest long-term strategy there is. Patients who feel rushed find other providers. Patients who feel cared for stay forever and refer everyone they know. You've clearly figured that out. Whatever comes next for the practice, that principle will keep guiding you well.
